topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music swelled, a vibrant wave washing over the crowded reception hall. I was nursing a lukewarm glass of champagne, trying to look engaged in the conversation with Sarah, when a familiar silhouette caught my eye across the room. It was him. Mark. He was standing near the buffet, laughing with someone, and for a split second, the air seemed to thin around my chest. My hand tightened around the glass until my knuckles were white. Every muscle in my body seemed to lock up. I desperately tried to pivot, to melt into the crowd, but my feet felt cemented to the polished floor. His gaze swept the room, and it snagged on mine. The laughter died in his throat, replaced by a sudden, sharp stillness. I could feel a distinct prickling sensation crawling up my neck. A sudden, urgent need to disappear washed over me; I mumbled something about needing air and bolted toward the exit, the sound of his voice echoing hollowly behind me.

[story 2]

I’d spent the entire afternoon rehearsing small talk, building a fortress of polite indifference around my heart. Then, the entrance of the wedding party announced the arrival of the one person I’d been actively avoiding. He materialized near the entrance, and the ambient noise of the celebration seemed to drop out entirely. My breath hitched, shallow and fast. I watched him approach, and the world narrowed down to the space between us. My palms grew slick, and I instinctively took a half-step back, bumping against a nearby pillar. A strange, cold pressure settled deep in my stomach, making my insides churn. When his eyes finally met mine, there was a flicker—a recognition, perhaps regret—that sent a jolt of pure alarm through me. I gripped my clutch bag so tightly I thought the leather might tear, my focus entirely fixed on the distance I needed to put between us, my mouth suddenly dry and refusing to form any coherent sound.

[story 3]

The clinking of glasses and the joyful din of the celebration were overwhelming, a relentless sensory assault. I was trying to enjoy the atmosphere, to feel connected to the happy chaos, but then I saw him. Standing by the floral arrangements, looking impossibly composed, was Alex. Time seemed to distort; the background noise faded into a dull hum. A wave of intense physical tension washed over me, making my vision swim slightly at the edges. I felt a sudden, desperate urge to retreat, to find any shadow or corner that offered sanctuary. My heart began to hammer against my ribs with a frantic, uneven rhythm. I forced myself to take a slow, deliberate breath, trying to regulate the frantic energy surging through my limbs, but the urge to flee was becoming overwhelming, a primal need to vanish from that immediate proximity.
